metadata.title: Build Your Website, Part 1: Pick a Platform & Get a First Draft
metadata.playlist: Ship It — Build Your Brand's Live Presence
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 8 brief — Building Your Live Website, Resumes & LinkedIn Header

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 1. Pick a platform. Write one brand-first prompt. Get a live draft. That's the whole job today. By the end of this video you'll have four real sections on screen — Hero, Projects, About, Contact — in your actual brand colors, with your actual font. Let's go.

props.segment: Build Your Website, Part 1: Pick a Platform & Get a First Draft
props.command: Pick a platform. Write one brand-first prompt. Ship a live draft.
output: 4 required sections: Hero · Projects · About · Contact
Your palette hex codes applied from prompt line one.
One revision pass. Then you're done with Part 1.

beats.narration_text: Four platforms will handle this assignment. v0.dev gives you the most control because it generates real React code you can inspect and edit — ideal if you're comfortable reading code, even a little. Framer AI is the designer-friendly option: drag-and-drop after generation, excellent typography defaults, no code required. Wix AI has the most guardrails — it asks you questions before generating, so it's harder to end up with something completely off-brand. Squarespace AI is the easiest to publish — one click and you have a live URL. The decision rule is this: pick the one that matches your current comfort level with code, and then stay there. Time spent switching platforms is time not building. The platform is not the assignment — the four sections are.

shot.new_visual_element: Platform decision matrix: four options (v0.dev, Framer AI, Wix AI, Squarespace AI) as a 2×2 grid with Code Control vs. Ease axes and one-line decision rule per platform

platforms.name: Squarespace AI
platforms.for: Just need a live URL fast

beats.narration_text: Now here is the prompt you paste. Do not type a generic prompt like 'make me a portfolio site.' That gives the tool permission to invent a color scheme and a font and a fake name. You own the brand — put it in line one. Fill in every placeholder in brackets: your brand name, your role, your value proposition, your background hex code, your accent hex code, your font name. The four required sections go in explicitly: Hero with your name, your role, and a call-to-action button; Projects with three items, each needing an image and a description; About; and Contact. Paste this entire prompt and press Enter. Do not break it into multiple messages — the tool needs the full context up front.

props.command: Build a personal portfolio site for [BRAND_NAME], a [ROLE] whose value proposition is [VALUE_PROP]. Brand palette: background [PRIMARY_HEX], accent [ACCENT_HEX], body font [FONT_NAME]. Required sections in this order: (1) Hero — my name, my role, and a CTA button labeled 'See My Work' that scrolls to Projects; (2) Projects — exactly 3 items, each with a placeholder image slot, a project title, and a 2-sentence description; (3) About — 3–4 sentences in first person about my background and approach; (4) Contact — email link and LinkedIn URL. Apply the palette and font consistently. Do not invent colors, names, or fonts not listed here.

beats.act: read the result against your brand
beats.narration_text: When the draft appears, do not ask yourself 'does this look nice.' Ask yourself whether it matches what you specified. Go through the checklist in order. First: are the hex codes applied — meaning background and accent — or did the tool invent a different shade? Second: is your font actually used for headings and body text, or did it fall back to a system sans-serif? Third: are all four sections present — Hero, Projects, About, Contact — in that order? Fourth: is the call-to-action button visible in the Hero section? Fifth: are there any remaining placeholder strings like 'Lorem ipsum,' 'Your Name Here,' or 'Insert text'? If you can check all five and the answer is yes, yes, yes, yes, no — you have a valid draft. If any answer is wrong, note what it is, because that goes into the revision prompt in the next beat.

props.artifactHeading: Read the result against your brand — not aesthetics
artifactLines: ✓  Hex codes applied — background [PRIMARY_HEX] and accent [ACCENT_HEX] visible, no invented shades.
✓  Font used — your specified font on headings and body, not a fallback system font.
✓  4 sections present — Hero, Projects, About, Contact in that order.
✓  CTA visible — 'See My Work' button in the Hero section.
✗  No placeholder text — zero instances of 'Lorem ipsum', 'Your Name Here', or 'Insert text'.
props.sparkLine: Match the spec, not the vibe.

beats.narration_text: One revision pass — not five. Pick the two or three specific things the checklist flagged and write them as one prompt. Here is the pattern: name the exact element that's wrong, say what it should be, and give the correct value from your brand sheet. For example: 'The hero background is using a gray — change it to [PRIMARY_HEX]. The Projects and About sections are merged into one block — separate them into two distinct sections with their own headings.' That is a good revision prompt because it is specific. 'Make it look better' is not a revision prompt — it gives the tool nothing to work with. After you submit the revision, run through the checklist one more time. If it passes, you are done with Part 1.

props.command: Two fixes: (1) The hero background is showing as gray — change it to [PRIMARY_HEX] exactly. (2) The Projects section and About section are merged — separate them into two visually distinct sections with their own headings and padding. Keep everything else the same.

beats.narration_text: Four traps that show up in almost every first submission. Trap one: blank placeholder boxes in the Projects section — visible empty image frames with no content. These are never acceptable. The assignment requires three project items with images and descriptions. Fill them with your actual work, or real descriptions, before submitting. Trap two: letting the tool pick your colors. If you did not include your hex codes in the first prompt, the tool invented a palette. That palette is not your brand. Go back, add the hex codes to the prompt, regenerate. Trap three: no call-to-action button in the Hero. The Hero section requires a CTA — a button the visitor can click. If it is missing, the section is incomplete. Trap four: three project cards with titles but no images. Each project item needs an image slot — a real image or at minimum a placeholder image, not an empty gray box. Catch all four before you move to Part 2.

artifactLines: Trap 1 — Blank placeholder boxes: visible empty image frames are never acceptable; fill Projects with real content.
Trap 2 — Tool-invented colors: if your hex codes weren't in prompt line one, the palette is fiction; regenerate with the correct codes.
Trap 3 — Missing CTA: the Hero section requires a visible call-to-action button — 'See My Work' or equivalent.
Trap 4 — Project titles, no images: each of the 3 project cards needs an image slot populated, not an empty gray rectangle.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Part 1. The brand-first prompt does three things in one shot: it locks in your palette hex codes, your font name, and the four required sections — Hero, Projects, About, Contact. After generation, you read the result against the brand spec, not against how it looks. One revision pass fixes the two or three specific things that didn't match. Then you stop. A functional first draft beats a beautiful one you're still tweaking when the deadline hits.

artifactLines: Brand-first prompt = palette hex codes + font name + 4 explicit sections in one message.
Read the draft against the spec: hex codes, font, 4 sections, CTA, no placeholder text.
One revision pass — specific element, specific fix, specific value from your brand sheet.
Functional first draft beats a beautiful unpublished mockup every time.

beats.narration_text: Your Turn. Open your Assignment 7 brand sheet and copy out your background hex code, your accent hex code, and your font name — have them ready before you open any platform. Then paste this complete prompt. Replace every bracketed placeholder with your real values. Submit it as one message — not piece by piece. After the draft appears, run the five-point checklist: hex codes, font, four sections, CTA, no placeholder text. If something is off, write a specific revision prompt naming the element, what's wrong, and what the correct value is. Submit it once. Then stop. You now have a draft. Part 2 is mobile and publish.
